reference: GATE CS — Discrete Mathematics / Graph Theory

map.condition: Cycle graph with n vertices (even n)
Cycle graph with n vertices (odd n)

formulas.name: Chromatic Number Definition
formulas.latex: \chi(G) = \text{minimum number of colours needed to colour vertices of } G \text{ so no two adjacent vertices share a colour}
formulas.plain: chi(G) = minimum colours so no two adjacent vertices share a colour
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever the question asks for the minimum number of colours required for proper vertex colouring.
formulas.trap: Chromatic number is the MINIMUM; you can always use more colours — the question is how few suffice.

formulas.name: Proper Vertex Colouring
formulas.latex: c: V \to \{1,2,\ldots,k\} \text{ is proper iff } \forall (u,v) \in E,\ c(u) \neq c(v)
formulas.plain: Assignment c: V -> {1..k} is proper iff for every edge (u,v), c(u) != c(v)
formulas.whenToUse: To verify or describe what a valid colouring means.
formulas.trap: Non-adjacent vertices CAN share a colour — only adjacent vertices must differ.

formulas.name: Even Cycle Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: chi(C_n) = 2 when n is even (n >= 4)
formulas.whenToUse: Colouring a cycle graph where the number of vertices is even.
formulas.trap: An even cycle is bipartite, so 2 colours suffice; do not confuse with odd cycles.

formulas.name: Odd Cycle Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: chi(C_n) = 3 when n is odd (n >= 3)
formulas.whenToUse: Colouring a cycle graph where the number of vertices is odd.
formulas.trap: An odd cycle is NOT bipartite; 2 colours always fail, so 3 are needed.

formulas.name: Complete Graph Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: chi(K_n) = n
formulas.whenToUse: Colouring K_n where every vertex is adjacent to every other vertex.
formulas.trap: Every vertex needs a distinct colour because all pairs are adjacent — do not apply this to sparse graphs.

formulas.name: Bipartite Graph Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: Bipartite non-trivial graph => chi(G) = 2
formulas.whenToUse: Graph has vertices partitioned into two independent sets (no odd cycles).

terms.means: bipartite graph with at least one edge

formulas.trap: A graph with no edges has chi = 1 (empty graph); a non-trivial bipartite graph needs exactly 2.

formulas.name: Tree / Forest Chromatic Number
formulas.latex: T \text{ is a tree (or forest) with } \geq 1 \text{ edge} \Rightarrow \chi(T) = 2
formulas.plain: Tree or forest with at least one edge => chi = 2
formulas.whenToUse: Graph is acyclic (tree or forest) with at least one edge.
formulas.trap: Trees are bipartite, so they always need exactly 2 colours (never 3).

formulas.name: Hypercube Graph Q_n Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: chi(Q_n) = 2 for the n-dimensional hypercube graph
formulas.whenToUse: Vertices are binary strings of length n, edges between strings differing in exactly one bit (hypercube / n-cube).
formulas.trap: Q_n is bipartite (partition by parity of number of 1-bits), so 2 colours always suffice regardless of n.

formulas.name: Four Colour Theorem (Planar Graphs)
formulas.plain: Any planar graph can be coloured with at most 4 colours
formulas.whenToUse: Graph can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings.
formulas.trap: 4 is the upper bound; the actual chi may be 1, 2, or 3 for specific planar graphs.

formulas.name: Wheel Graph Chromatic Number
formulas.latex: \chi(W_n) = \begin{cases} 3 & n \text{ even (outer cycle odd length)} \\ 4 & n \text{ odd (outer cycle even length)} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: W_n (n outer vertices): chi = 3 if n is even, chi = 4 if n is odd
formulas.whenToUse: Wheel graph W_n: hub vertex connected to all n rim vertices forming a cycle.

terms.means: wheel graph with n rim vertices and 1 hub

formulas.trap: The hub is adjacent to every rim vertex, raising chi by at least 1 above the rim cycle's chi.

formulas.name: Clique Lower Bound
formulas.plain: chi(G) >= omega(G)  [clique number is a lower bound]
formulas.whenToUse: When a graph contains a large clique, use its size as a lower bound on chi.

terms.means: clique number (size of the largest clique in G)

formulas.trap: The bound can be strict — there are graphs (e.g., Mycielski graphs) with large chi but omega = 2.

formulas.name: Delta Upper Bound
formulas.plain: chi(G) <= Delta(G) + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Quick upper bound when only the maximum degree is known (Greedy / Brooks' theorem basis).

terms.means: maximum degree of any vertex in G

formulas.trap: Brooks' theorem tightens this to chi <= Delta for all connected graphs except K_n and odd cycles.

formulas.name: Brooks' Theorem
formulas.plain: chi(G) <= Delta(G) for connected graphs that are not complete or odd cycle
formulas.whenToUse: Tighter upper bound on chromatic number for any connected graph other than K_n or odd cycles.
formulas.trap: The two exceptions (K_n and odd cycles) exactly require Delta + 1 colours.

formulas.name: Independence Number Lower Bound
formulas.plain: chi(G) >= ceil(|V| / alpha(G))
formulas.whenToUse: When the independence number alpha(G) is known, derive a lower bound on chi.

terms.means: independence number (size of largest independent set)

formulas.trap: This bound may not be tight; use it together with the clique lower bound for the best estimate.

formulas.name: Chromatic Polynomial Definition
formulas.latex: P(G, k) = \text{number of proper colourings of } G \text{ using exactly up to } k \text{ colours}
formulas.plain: P(G, k) = number of proper colourings of G with at most k colours
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many valid colourings exist with k colours.
formulas.trap: P(G, chi(G)) may be 0 if chi(G) colours are needed but k < chi(G); chi is the smallest k where P > 0.

formulas.name: Deletion-Contraction Recurrence
formulas.latex: P(G, k) = P(G - e, k) - P(G / e, k)
formulas.plain: P(G, k) = P(G minus edge e, k) - P(G contract edge e, k)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing chromatic polynomial recursively by removing one edge at a time.

terms.means: graph G with edge e deleted
graph G with edge e contracted (endpoints merged)

formulas.trap: Apply to ONE edge per step; always choose an edge, not a vertex, for deletion/contraction.

formulas.name: Chromatic Polynomial of K_n
formulas.plain: P(K_n, k) = k * (k-1) * (k-2) * ... * (k-n+1)  [falling factorial]
formulas.whenToUse: Complete graph: each vertex must get a distinct colour.
formulas.trap: This equals n! when k = n, confirming n! proper colourings with exactly n colours for K_n.

formulas.name: Chromatic Polynomial of a Tree (n vertices)
formulas.whenToUse: Any tree on n vertices: root can take k colours, each subsequent vertex avoids its parent's colour.

terms.means: number of edges = n-1 for a tree

formulas.trap: This applies to ANY tree structure, not just paths — the formula is the same regardless of tree shape.

formulas.name: Chromatic Polynomial of C_n
formulas.whenToUse: Counting proper colourings of a cycle C_n with k colours.

terms.means: sign depends on parity of n

formulas.trap: For n=3 (triangle/K_3): P = k(k-1)(k-2), consistent with the formula — verify by substitution.

formulas.name: Edge Chromatic Number (Chromatic Index)
formulas.latex: \chi'(G) = \text{minimum colours to colour edges so no two edges sharing a vertex have the same colour}
formulas.plain: chi'(G) = minimum colours for edges so incident edges get distinct colours
formulas.whenToUse: When the question is about colouring EDGES (not vertices) of a graph.
formulas.trap: Edge colouring != vertex colouring. chi'(G) = Delta or Delta+1 (Vizing's theorem), never less than Delta.

formulas.name: Vizing's Theorem
formulas.plain: Delta(G) <= chi'(G) <= Delta(G) + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding the chromatic index of any simple graph; chi' is either Delta or Delta+1.
formulas.trap: Bipartite graphs are always Class 1 (chi' = Delta); K_n with n odd is Class 2 (chi' = Delta+1 = n).

formulas.name: Bipartite iff 2-Colourable
formulas.latex: G \text{ is bipartite} \iff \chi(G) \leq 2 \iff G \text{ has no odd-length cycle}
formulas.plain: G is bipartite <=> chi(G) <= 2 <=> G has no odd cycle
formulas.whenToUse: Determining if a graph needs only 2 colours, or checking if it is bipartite.
formulas.trap: A single odd cycle anywhere in the graph forces chi >= 3 for the entire graph.

formulas.name: k-Colourable / k-Chromatic
formulas.latex: G \text{ is } k\text{-colourable} \iff \chi(G) \leq k; \quad G \text{ is } k\text{-chromatic} \iff \chi(G) = k
formulas.plain: G is k-colourable iff chi(G) <= k; G is k-chromatic iff chi(G) = k exactly
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing 'can be coloured with k colours' from 'requires exactly k colours'.

terms.means: exactly k colours are the minimum

formulas.trap: k-colourable does NOT mean exactly k colours are needed — it's an upper bound condition.

formulas.name: Five Colour Theorem
formulas.latex: G \text{ planar} \Rightarrow \chi(G) \leq 5 \quad (\text{easier to prove than 4-colour theorem})
formulas.plain: Every planar graph is 5-colourable (weaker but provable without computer)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about the historically proven bound before the four-colour theorem was verified.
formulas.trap: The four-colour theorem (chi <= 4 for planar) is the tighter result; 5-colour is only a stepping stone.
